petitioner_background: The petitioner is a self-represented Chinese national seeking classification as an employment-based immigrant under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act as an alien of extraordinary ability in the field of agriculture (agriculturist). According to the Form I-140, the petitioner has been residing in the United States since October 1, 1999, and his Chinese passport was issued in New York on February 14, 2000. The petition was filed on May 4, 2005, more than five years after his arrival in the United States. In support of the petition, he submitted only a signed statement regarding his qualifications as an agriculturist.
outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner failed to respond to the director's June 22, 2005 notice of intent to deny, which had specifically requested evidence pertaining to the regulatory criteria at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3). Although the petitioner submitted evidence pertaining to those criteria for the first time on appeal, the AAO declined to consider it, citing Matter of Soriano and Matter of Obaigbena, which hold that evidence offered for the first time on appeal will not be accepted when the petitioner was previously put on notice and given a reasonable opportunity to provide it. Based on the record before the director, the petitioner failed to demonstrate that he met at least three of the regulatory criteria or that he had achieved sustained national or international acclaim placing him within the small percentage at the very top of his field. As an independent and alternative basis for denial, the AAO also found that the record contained no evidence satisfying 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(5), which requires clear evidence that the alien is coming to the United States to continue work in the area of expertise.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Spencer Enterprises, Inc. v. United States, 229 F. Supp. 2d 1025, 1043 (E.D. Cal. 2001), aff'd, 345 F.3d 683 (9th Cir. 2003)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issue: The petitioner failed to respond to the director's June 22, 2005 notice of intent to deny, which requested evidence pertaining to the criteria at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3). The petitioner then attempted to submit the requested evidence for the first time on appeal. The AAO refused to consider this evidence, applying the rule from Matter of Soriano and Matter of Obaigbena that evidence offered for the first time on appeal will not be accepted where the petitioner was previously put on notice and given a reasonable opportunity to provide it. The appeal was therefore adjudicated solely on the record of proceeding before the director.
